questions.prompt: A client emails: 'we're moving our website to a new host this Saturday.' The four-layer lookup shows: GoDaddy (registrar), GoDaddy DNS (DNS host), VPS (web host), M365 (mail host). Which transfer is this?
choices: All three transfers; moving the website implies moving everything.
questions.explanation: Only the A (and AAAA, if present) records change to point at the new web host. The registrar, DNS host, and mail host stay put. Web host migration changes the A/AAAA values; nothing else. Mail at M365 stays untouched, the registrar doesn't change, the DNS host doesn't change. The 'move means everything' assumption is the documented misconception.

questions.prompt: A client says 'we're consolidating with one provider — they sell hosting, DNS, and email.' What does that imply you'll touch, before you've clarified?
choices: All four layers (registrar, DNS host, web host, mail host) will likely change.
Three layers (DNS host, web host, mail host) likely change;
Only the web host changes; consolidation never touches mail or DNS.
Refuse — consolidation is too risky to attempt.
questions.explanation: The registrar might or might not. Confirm whether the new provider is also taking over the domain registration before quoting. Hosting/DNS/email consolidation usually means three layer changes (DNS host, web host, mail host). Whether the registrar moves too is a separate question. 'All four' isn't safe to assume; many consolidations keep the registrar with the existing provider for cost or lock-in reasons. Always confirm the registrar layer explicitly before quoting.

questions.prompt: A client wants all four layers moved to a new all-in-one provider. What's the safer approach?
choices: Do everything at once on cutover day for minimum disruption.
Registrar transfer first; the others follow automatically.
Refuse; the layers can't all move at once.
questions.explanation: Then record-level migrations for web and mail, then registrar transfer. Verify after each step. Parallel execution means a failure during cutover takes more time to diagnose. Sequencing with verification between each step catches problems early. Each layer is independent; registrar transfer doesn't auto-move DNS or hosting.
